talk_metadata.talk_title: GPNMB-targeted dual-compartment CAR T cells for glioblastoma
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "most CAR T-cell trials in glioblastoma have failed to produce long-standing remission"
Speaker admitted: "we did see GPNMB antigen escape"
Speaker noted: "the ADC was pulled from trial due to skin toxicity"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Potential antigen loss variants not addressed by single antigen targeting
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: We did see GPNMB antigen escape in the few mice that succumbed
vulnerability_identified.ghost_responder_admission: Most mice had durable clearance, but a few escaped

key_data_gaps: No orthotopic immunocompetent efficacy beyond mouse models
No human safety or toxicity data

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO could model GPNMB antigen loss and predict alternative targets or combination strategies to prevent escape.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker admitted antigen escape in a subset of mice.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Use computational modeling of tumor heterogeneity to design multi‑antigen CAR constructs.

crispro_opportunity.description: Identify patients with high GPNMB expression and low likelihood of antigen loss for enrollment.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker showed GPNMB up‑regulation in recurrent GBM and absent in normal neural cells.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Integrate transcriptomic datasets to develop a predictive GPNMB expression score.

cited_competitors.name: blenzetumumab ADC
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as a prior GPNMB‑targeting ADC that failed due to skin toxicity.

cited_competitors.name: CDX011 CAR
cited_competitors.context: Referenced as a GPNMB CAR used in metastatic sarcoma with good tolerance.

unresolved_questions: What is the durability of response in an immunocompetent GBM model?
Can combination with checkpoint blockade overcome residual immunosuppression?

talk_metadata.talk_title: Tumor‑targeted lipid nanoparticles delivering IL‑12 circRNA for lung cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Linear mRNA has three fundamental limitations that makes this difficult"
Speaker noted: "IL‑12 systemic administration is associated with serious toxicity"
Speaker emphasized: "Local circRNA delivery preserves efficacy without a toxicity signal"

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Lipid library is disclosed; core scaffold may be replicable by competitors
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Requires high‑purity circRNA production via size‑exclusion HPLC
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Relies on intratumoral or intratracheal injection, limiting systemic applicability

key_data_gaps: No comparison to standard of care IL‑12 protein

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO can identify tumors with high IL‑12 receptor expression to prioritize patients for circRNA therapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted need for tumor‑localized IL‑12 to avoid systemic toxicity.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Predictive modeling of IL‑12 pathway activation across lung cancer cohorts.

crispro_opportunity.description: Demonstrate computational advantage of circRNA stability over linear mRNA for sustained cytokine expression.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker showed kinetic advantage of circRNA versus mRNA in vitro and in vivo.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Model kinetic profiles to optimize dosing schedules.

cited_competitors.name: Recombinant IL‑12 protein
cited_competitors.context: Cited as having severe systemic toxicity limiting clinical translation.

unresolved_questions: What is the biodistribution of the lipid nanoparticle after intratracheal delivery?
Can the platform be adapted for other cytokines or tumor types?

rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "We avoid many of the common resistance mechanisms by targeting a TAM rather than a mutating cancer cell receptor"
Speaker noted: "We did not see toxicity at all in the liver"
Speaker admitted: "We saw some mice with tumor regrowth and suspect MDR1 efflux"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Potential MDR1‑mediated drug efflux in tumor cells reducing MMAE efficacy
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: We suspect that MDR1 and drug efflux pumps might be a problem

trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: All‑comers without biomarker selection may dilute signal in low‑TAM tumors
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential underpowering if TAM prevalence varies widely

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Described as 1.4 DAR polymer; size and chemistry could be replicated by competitors
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Requires precise DAR control and rapid clearance kinetics
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Broad all‑comers design may limit early efficacy signals

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed robust tumor regression in subcutaneous models but later admitted limited survival benefit in intracranial glioblastoma, yet still concluded the platform is broadly effective

key_data_gaps: Limited data on CNS penetration beyond mouse models

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO could identify synergistic DNA‑damage agents to pair with MAC‑TACs to overcome MDR1‑mediated resistance.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker mentioned MDR1 as a suspected resistance mechanism.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Predict drug‑drug interaction networks for TAM‑delivered payloads.

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a predictive model of TAM receptor density to select patients most likely to benefit.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker emphasized that more TAMs = better efficacy.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Integrate IHC quantification data with transcriptomics to generate a TAM‑score.

cited_competitors.name: MMAE ADCs
cited_competitors.context: Compared to conventional ADCs, MAC‑TACs are smaller and clear faster.

unresolved_questions: What is the optimal dosing interval for different tumor types?
Can the platform be adapted for hematologic malignancies?

rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "Collagen I and III are minimally expressed in normal brain but strongly up‑regulated in recurrent GBM"
Speaker noted: "CBD anti‑CTLA‑4 extended survival by about 79% in an intracranial model"
Speaker admitted: "We did not see a complete cure, only survival extension"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Potential limited penetration into deep tumor regions despite collagen binding

trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: All‑comers without selection for high collagen I/III expression may dilute efficacy
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential underpowering if only subset expresses target collagen

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: Von Willebrand factor A3 collagen‑binding domain
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Domain is derived from a natural protein; may be freely usable after licensing
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Requires conjugation of CBD to antibody without affecting Fc function
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Relies on collagen exposure which may vary across patients

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed strong accumulation of CBD antibody in tumor but later reported only modest survival benefit, suggesting a mismatch between pharmacokinetic advantage and therapeutic outcome

key_data_gaps: No head‑to‑head comparison with standard anti‑CTLA‑4
Limited safety data on liver accumulation

crispro_opportunity.description: Develop a predictive model of intratumoral collagen I/III levels to select patients for CBD anti‑CTLA‑4 therapy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted collagen I/III up‑regulation in recurrent GBM.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Integrate RNA‑seq and imaging data to generate a collagen‑score.

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO could suggest synergistic checkpoint combinations (e.g., CBD PD‑1) to enhance efficacy.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker discussed potential to combine with PD‑1/PD‑L1 blockade.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Model immune network effects of dual collagen‑binding checkpoints.

cited_competitors.name: Standard anti‑CTLA‑4 antibodies
cited_competitors.context: Used as control showing poor brain accumulation.

unresolved_questions: What is the long‑term safety of collagen binding in non‑tumor tissues (e.g., liver)?
Can the CBD approach be applied to other checkpoint inhibitors?

rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "CD2 is constitutively expressed and increased in exhausted T cells"
Speaker noted: "We see enhanced cytotoxicity at two orders of magnitude lower dose than tarlatamab"
Speaker admitted: "We have not yet observed toxicity in NHPs but expect CRS similar to other TCEs"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Potential for cytokine release syndrome at higher doses despite low‑dose efficacy
vulnerability_identified.hidden_tox_signal: We expect CRS but have not seen it yet in preclinical models

trial_dilution_risk.trial_name_or_nct: Planned first‑in‑human DLL3 trispecific study (no NCT yet)
trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: DLL3 expression on small‑cell lung cancer
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: All‑comers without DLL3 expression threshold may dilute response
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential underpowering if DLL3 low‑expressors are included

competitive_moat_weakness.vulnerable_ip_or_chemistry: CD3 VHH domain and 2‑by‑2‑by‑2 trispecific format
competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: VHH and format may be engineered by competitors; not heavily protected
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Complex multi‑domain assembly may affect yield
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Relies on DLL3, a target with heterogeneous expression

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker demonstrated markedly lower cytokine release at efficacious doses yet still warned that CRS will be comparable to tarlatamab, indicating a mismatch between data and safety narrative

key_data_gaps: No human safety or PK data
Limited data on long‑term T‑cell persistence

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO can model DLL3 down‑regulation and suggest alternative co‑stimulatory domains.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker noted that tarlatamab loses activity over serial challenges while trispecific retains.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Simulate antigen loss scenarios and predict efficacy of CD2 co‑stimulation.

crispro_opportunity.description: Incorporate DLL3 expression biomarker thresholds into early‑phase trial design.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker emphasized need for DLL3‑positive tumors for activity.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Optimize inclusion criteria using predictive modeling of DLL3 prevalence.

cited_competitors.name: tarlatamab (Imdelata)
cited_competitors.context: Used as benchmark comparator for efficacy and cytokine release.

unresolved_questions: What is the maximum tolerated dose in humans?
Does CD2 engagement affect T‑cell trafficking or tissue retention?

talk_metadata.talk_title: CBP/P300 bromodomain inhibition combined with PARP inhibition in prostate cancer
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "CBP/P300 amplifications are enriched in castration‑resistant prostate cancer"
Speaker noted: "We observed moderate to high synergy between CCS1477 and all four PARP inhibitors"
Speaker admitted: "Response heterogeneity in patient‑derived explants suggests biomarkers are needed"

vulnerability_identified.mechanistic_blindspot: Lack of predictive biomarkers to identify responders among heterogeneous PDEs
vulnerability_identified.unexplained_resistance_quote: Some PDEs were unresponsive to either single agent or combination

trial_dilution_risk.target_biology: CBP/P300 bromodomain inhibition plus PARP‑mediated DNA repair blockade
trial_dilution_risk.flawed_enrollment_criteria: All‑comers without DDR biomarker selection may dilute efficacy
trial_dilution_risk.missing_biomarker: BRCA1/2 loss or HR deficiency signature
trial_dilution_risk.statistical_concern: Potential underpowering if only a minority are HR‑deficient

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Bromodomain inhibitors are a crowded space; scaffold may be replicated
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Requires precise dosing to avoid overlapping toxicities with PARP inhibitors

cognitive_dissonance: Speaker showed strong preclinical synergy yet emphasized that many PDEs did not respond, indicating a gap between mechanistic rationale and translational efficacy

key_data_gaps: In vivo efficacy data not presented
Safety profile of combination in patients

crispro_opportunity.description: CrisPRO can integrate copy‑number and exome data to derive a predictive HR‑deficiency score for the combination.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker highlighted copy‑number gain/loss differences between responders and non‑responders.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Machine‑learning model to predict synergy based on genomic alterations.

crispro_opportunity.description: Suggest rational addition of immune checkpoint blockade to exploit increased mutational load from DNA repair inhibition.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker asked about combining with checkpoint inhibition.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Simulate tumor‑immune interactions under combined DDR and checkpoint inhibition.

cited_competitors.name: PARP inhibitors (olaparib, rucaparib, niraparib, talazoparib)
cited_competitors.context: Combined with CCS1477 in synergy screens.

unresolved_questions: What is the optimal dosing schedule to minimize overlapping toxicities?
Can the combination be effective in HR‑proficient tumors?

talk_metadata.institution_or_pharma: Cancer Research Center of Lyon / Anticeneo CRO
talk_metadata.talk_title: 3D bioprinted cancer models for drug screening
rhetorical_signals: Speaker said: "3D bioprinted models share more characteristics with in vivo tumors than 2D models"
Speaker noted: "IC values are higher in 3D than 2D, indicating reduced drug sensitivity"
Speaker admitted: "Co‑culture with fibroblasts or adipocytes reduces drug sensitivity further"

competitive_moat_weakness.ip_vulnerability_note: Process steps (bioink formulation, extrusion) are broadly known; limited proprietary protection
competitive_moat_weakness.scale_or_manufacturing_bottleneck: Requires specialized bioprinters and HPLC‑purified cell lines for reproducibility
competitive_moat_weakness.clinical_strategy_weakness: Model lacks vasculature, limiting pharmacokinetic relevance

key_data_gaps: No in vivo validation of drug response predictions

crispro_opportunity.description: Use CrisPRO to integrate 3D model drug response data with patient genomic profiles for adaptive trial enrichment.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker emphasized need for better preclinical models to improve confidence.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Predict which patient sub‑cohorts will align with 3D model sensitivity patterns.

crispro_opportunity.description: Demonstrate computational advantage of 3D bioprinting over 2D screens in predicting in vivo pharmacokinetics.
crispro_opportunity.transcript_evidence: Speaker showed delayed drug uptake kinetics in 3D matching in vivo data.
crispro_opportunity.crispro_angle: Model diffusion and metabolism to refine dosing schedules.

cited_competitors.name: Standard 2D cell culture
cited_competitors.context: Compared unfavorably in gene expression and drug sensitivity.

cited_competitors.name: Mouse xenograft models
cited_competitors.context: Mentioned as costly and less human‑relevant.

unresolved_questions: How will incorporation of microfluidic vasculature affect drug penetration predictions?
Can the platform be scaled for high‑throughput screening?

assets_to_track: 3D bioprinted HER2+ breast and ovarian cancer models
